title: Admission Day Monument (1897) – Pioneer Monuments in the American West
content: # Pioneer Monuments in the American West. # Admission Day Monument (1897). Admission Day Monument in San Francisco in 2013. Also known as the Native Sons Monument and the Phelan Fountain, the Admission Day Monument was erected to celebrate California’s 1850 admission to the Union. It was commissioned by San Francisco Mayor James D. It was unveiled on California Admission Day (September 9) in 1897. At the base of the column stands a miner representing the Youth of California. The miner also honors the Native Sons of the Golden West, to whom the monument is dedicated. It is engraved with a declaration made by W.H. Seward to U.S. Senate on California’s admission to the Union: “The unity of our empire hangs on the decision of this day.”. The Admission Day monument was intended as a public drinking fountain. The monument stood at the intersection of Mason, Turk and Market Streets, near the Native Sons of the Golden West in San Francisco’s Tenderloin District, from 1897-1948.

title: Admission Day Monument - Wikipedia
content: | Location | Market Street "Market Street (San Francisco)") and Montgomery Street in San Francisco, California, United States |. The ***Admission Day Monument*** is an 1897 sculpture by Douglas Tilden, located at the intersection of Market Street "Market Street (San Francisco)") and Montgomery Street in San Francisco, California, United States. Other names for the sculpture include ***Admission Day***, the ***Admission Day Fountain***, the ***California Admissions Day Monument***, ***Native Son Monument***, ***Native Sons' Monument***, ***Native Sons Monument***, and ***Phelan Fountain***. The monument was unveiled on September 5, 1897 at Market, Turk and Mason Streets, and dedicated to the Native Sons of the Golden West on September 9, 1897. GENTLEMEN: I hereby tender the City and County of San Francisco a fountain constructed of marble and bronze, emblematic of the Admission of California into the Union, designed by Douglas Tilden, sculptor, to be erected by me, with your consent, at the junction of Market, Turk and Mason streets and to be forever maintained by the city as a drinking fountain.
